title: How the body develops chronic inflammation from sustained trauma
author: Kalesh
excerpt: You've probably heard the term "inflammation" thrown around a lot. Maybe your doctor mentioned it. Maybe you saw it in a wellness article. But here's the thing...
metaDescription: You've probably heard the term "inflammation" thrown around a lot. Maybe your doctor mentioned it. Maybe you saw it in a wellness article. But here's the...
htmlContent:
You've probably heard the term "inflammation" thrown around a lot. Maybe your doctor mentioned it. Maybe you saw it in a wellness article. But here's the thing: when we talk about chronic inflammation from sustained trauma, we're not just talking about a sprained ankle or a food sensitivity. We're talking about something that lives deep in your bones, in your cells, in the quiet hum of your nervous system that never quite shuts off.

I know what it's like to wake up feeling like your body is at war with itself. The ache in your joints. The fog in your brain. The way your stomach clenches for no reason you can name. You've tried the anti-inflammatory diets, the supplements, the yoga classes. And maybe they helped a little. But the underlying hum never really went away. Right?

That's because chronic inflammation from trauma isn't a diet problem. It's a nervous system problem. And until we understand that connection, we're just treating symptoms while the fire keeps burning.

The Nervous System and the Inflammatory Switch

Let me introduce you to something called the vagus nerve. It's the main highway between your brain and your body, and it's responsible for telling your immune system when to calm down. Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, spent decades mapping out how this nerve works. What he found is that when your nervous system perceives safety, the vagus nerve sends signals that dampen inflammation. But when your nervous system perceives danger, that signal gets blocked.

Here's where it gets personal. If you grew up in an unpredictable environment, if you experienced abuse or neglect, if you lived through something your body couldn't escape, your nervous system learned to keep the danger signal on. Not because something is wrong with you. Because your body was trying to keep you alive.

Think about it. Your immune system is designed to respond to threats with inflammation. A cut gets red and swollen. A virus triggers a fever. That's acute inflammation, and it's supposed to shut off once the threat is gone. But when your nervous system stays in a state of high alert, it keeps sending the signal that there's a threat, even when the threat is long gone. Your immune system keeps responding. And the inflammation becomes chronic.

I remember reading Gabor Mate's work on this. He talks about how the body holds the history of what we've lived through. He says that when we suppress our emotions, when we disconnect from our bodies to survive, we're telling our immune system to keep fighting a war that ended years ago. Wild, right? But it's not just emotional. It's biological. It's chemical. It's real.

How Trauma Reprograms Your Immune Cells

Stay with me here, because this gets a little technical, but I promise it matters. Your immune system has something called cytokines, which are like messengers that tell your cells to inflame or to heal. When you're under chronic stress from unresolved trauma, your body produces more pro-inflammatory cytokines and fewer anti-inflammatory ones. It's like your immune system gets stuck in the "on" position.

Bessel van der Kolk, the author of "The Body Keeps the Score," has shown through brain imaging that trauma literally changes the structure of your brain and the function of your immune system. He found that people with PTSD have higher levels of inflammatory markers like C-reactive protein and interleukin-6. These aren't just numbers on a lab report. They correlate with everything from heart disease to autoimmune conditions to depression.

And here's the thing that most people don't talk about: this inflammation isn't random. It tends to settle in the places where you hold your trauma. For some people, it's the gut. For others, it's the joints or the skin or the brain. I've worked with clients who developed rheumatoid arthritis after years of suppressing anger. I've known people whose chronic sinus infections cleared up when they started processing childhood neglect. The body doesn't lie.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. She breaks down how to actually shift your nervous system state, which is the first step in calming that inflammatory response. It's not about forcing yourself to relax. It's about giving your body evidence that it's safe now.

The Stress Hormone Loop That Keeps Inflammation Alive

Your body has a stress response system called the HPA axis. That's your hypothalamus, pituitary gland, and adrenal glands working together. When you're in danger, they release cortisol and adrenaline to help you fight, flee, or freeze. That's normal. That's healthy. The problem starts when you're stuck in that loop for months or years.

Here's what happens: chronic cortisol exposure actually makes your cells less sensitive to cortisol's anti-inflammatory effects. So your body keeps pumping out cortisol, but your cells stop listening. Meanwhile, your immune system is still producing inflammatory chemicals because it thinks the threat is still there. It's a feedback loop that's hard to break.

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma gets trapped in the body when the survival response is incomplete. If you couldn't run away or fight back, that energy stays frozen in your tissues. And that frozen energy keeps your nervous system on high alert, which keeps the inflammation going. It's not in your head. It's in your fascia, your muscles, your organs.

I've seen this in my own body. For years, I had chronic shoulder pain that no doctor could explain. I tried physical therapy, massage, acupuncture. Nothing stuck. It wasn't until I started working with the underlying trauma that the inflammation began to calm down. The shoulder pain wasn't the problem. It was the messenger.

A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No (paid link) by Gabor Mate. He walks through case after case showing how suppressed emotions and unprocessed trauma lead to chronic inflammatory conditions. It's not an easy read, but it's an honest one.

What Calming the Nervous System Actually Looks Like

So what do you do with all this? You don't just take an anti-inflammatory supplement and call it a day. You have to address the root cause, which is the nervous system's perception of threat. And that takes time. That takes patience. That takes a willingness to feel what you've been avoiding.

One of the most effective ways to start is by regulating your sensory environment. Your nervous system is constantly scanning for cues of safety or danger. If you're living in a loud, chaotic, overstimulating environment, your body will keep producing inflammatory markers. A discreet option for reducing noise sensitivity is Loop Quiet 2 Ear Plugs (paid link). They lower the volume of the world without blocking it out completely, which can help your nervous system settle.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link) by Peter Levine. He explains how trauma lives in the body and how to release it through gentle, titrated movement and awareness. It's not about reliving the trauma. It's about completing the survival response that got stuck.

Look, I'm not going to tell you that calming your nervous system will cure everything. That's the kind of toxic positivity that the wellness industry loves to sell. The truth is more complicated. Some inflammation becomes permanent. Some damage can't be undone. But that doesn't mean you're helpless. Your body is capable of healing when it feels safe enough to let its guard down.

Notice what just happened in your body when you read that. Did your shoulders drop? Did your breath deepen? That's your nervous system responding to the possibility of safety. That's the beginning of the shift.

Sit with that. Don't try to fix anything right now. Just notice.
